note: Archetypal CD2. All rules apply in full.
Only doctors (not nurse/pharmacist IPs) can prescribe for addiction treatment.
Patches retain drug post-use — patch disposal rules apply.
Common CD2 opioid. Full rules.
FP10MDA instalment Rx, daily collection, supervised consumption — unique rules cluster.
CD2 stimulant for ADHD. Tests CD2 rules apply beyond opioids.
Reclassified CD3 to CD2 in 2014. Classic exam trap.
Legitimate clinical use as topical anaesthetic (ENT/ophthalmology). Full CD2.
Palliative/anaesthesia. Tests CD2 rules on less familiar opioids.
Prodrug of amphetamine for ADHD. Candidates assume stimulants are less controlled.
Historical opioid. CD2 in full. Obstetric use contexts common.

MEP/legislation named. The ONE Schedule 2 CD specifically exempt from safe custody under the Safe Custody Regulations 1973. All other CD2 rules apply (register, W&F, witnessed destruction). High-yield exam point.
MEP class-level CD2 stimulant example. Distinct from lisdexamfetamine — both CD2.
Dentists, nurse IPs and pharmacist IPs cannot prescribe for drug misuse treatment.
Strong opioid in palliative care. Full CD2.
Newer opioid. Tests CD2 rules on unfamiliar names.
ADHD and narcolepsy. CD2. Often paired with methylphenidate in scenarios.
Synthetic cannabinoid for chemo nausea. CD2 — catches those who assume cannabis medicines are CD4.
Ultra-short opioid for anaesthesia. Hospital placement candidates encounter it. CD2.
Potent opioid anaesthetic alongside remifentanil and alfentanil. Completes the anaesthetic opioid trio.
Compound opium preparation. CD2. Surgical/older palliative care scenarios.

The only Schedule 3 benzo (others are CD4). Subject to safe custody and full Reg 15 prescription requirements (including W&F). Note: temazepam used to have a Reg 15 prescription exemption but this was removed in 2015.
Opioid substitution and pain. CD3. Safe custody yes, register no.

Most dispensed CD3. EXEMPT from safe custody (since 2014). All other CD3 prescription rules apply, including total quantity in words and figures. Common misconception: tramadol is NOT exempt from W&F.
Palliative/end-of-life. CD3 exempt from safe custody. Buccal use in paediatric epilepsy.
ONLY CD where emergency supply to patient is permitted (epilepsy). Exempt from safe custody.
Reclassified CD3 in 2019. Exempt from safe custody. All other CD3 prescription rules apply, including total quantity in words and figures.
Reclassified CD3 in 2019 alongside pregabalin. Exempt from safe custody. All other CD3 prescription rules apply, including total quantity in words and figures.
CD3 benzo (not CD4). Safe custody required.
Appetite suppressant. CD3. Safe custody required.
Archetypal CD4. Emergency supply permitted. Paramedics can independently administer. Repeat dispensing allowed.
CD4. Paramedics can administer independently. Acute seizure scenarios.
CD4. Alcohol detoxification — tests CD4 recognition outside psychiatric context.
CD4. Paediatric epilepsy and sleep disorders. MEP named benzo example.
CD4 Part 1 Z-drug. Not a benzo chemically but same controls.
CD4 Part 1 Z-drug alongside zopiclone and zaleplon.

MEP named. CD4 Part 1 cannabis spray. Pharmacists ARE required to keep records of Sativex; Home Office strongly recommends using the CD register for these records. Unique among CD4 drugs in having a record-keeping requirement.
CD4 benzo for epilepsy. Tests not all anticonvulsants are CD3.
CD4. Short-acting benzo. Anxiety/alcohol withdrawal.
CD4 Part 1. Adjunct epilepsy therapy. MEP named benzo.
Third Z-drug with zopiclone and zolpidem. CD4 Part 1.
CD4 Part 1. High public recognition (Xanax). Tests candidates know its schedule.
CD4 Part 1. In NHS formularies as a hypnotic.
CD4 Part 1. Short-acting benzo hypnotic. Appears in some NHS formularies.
CD4 Part 2. Tests Part 2 exists and differs from Part 1 benzos.
MEP named CD4 Part 2. Adrenoceptor stimulant — tests Part 2 is not only steroids.
CD5 OTC. Tests the CD2/CD5 dose boundary for codeine.

CD5 cough suppressant. WITHDRAWN from the UK market by MHRA in March 2023 due to risk of perianaesthetic anaphylaxis to neuromuscular blocking agents. Schedule classification remains CD5 in the legislation, but the drug is no longer available.
MEP named CD5. Low-dose codeine preparation. P medicine.
MEP named CD5. Low-strength preparation. Distinct from higher-dose codeine (CD2).
MEP named CD5. Low-concentration morphine — NOT the 20mg/ml concentrated solution which is CD2.
MEP named CD5. Dihydrocodeine slow-release preparation at low strength.
CD5 paracetamol/dihydrocodeine combination. Minimal controls but still scheduled.
CD5 as preparation. Higher-dose dihydrocodeine is CD2. Boundary question.
CBD oral solution for epilepsy. CD5. Tests cannabis-derived medicines span multiple schedules.
